Choppers have taken folks out of the country, or they were provided by foreign governments such as the U.S. government, the French government, and now the Canadian government that have been supplying choppers to come and get nationals. The Canadians are just some of the thousands of people who must be trying to get out of Haiti right now. What is the impact, do you think, of that migration, broadly of that migration? Well, listen, Haiti has been in a very complicated situation for the past few years, mainly with both an institutional and a constitutional crisis that got aggravated with the assassination of President Jovenel Moise in July of 2021. Now, we are facing today, and at least for the last two weeks, a total void of governance in the country, which is making things complicated to address the whole situation, whether security, whether the humanitarian catastrophe unfolding. It's's a very very complicated situation uh and it's triggering you know migration movements as you just said um as you know people are losing hope and they're trying to find a way out trying to find a better future somewhere else so you mentioned the governance i mean there is a plan for a transitional council to replace the prime minister who resigned and to move Haiti toward elections in the future. But hearing just today that that seems to be, that whole plan seems to be in jeopardy. Again, what's the latest you understand of that, Harold? It is complicated. We were promised a government within 48 hours a few weeks ago, and it's been two weeks now. I'm interested, finally, in your own situation. What are your own plans and what concerns do you have for your own personal safety, Harold? Well, I'm not just by myself. My family is here. Well, I wish you well as you stay put, and we'll be in touch hopefully in the future. Thank you very much, Harold, for that information and all of your insight tonight. Thanks for having me, Heather. Anticipation is building for a once-in-a-lifetime experience, next month's total solar eclipse. In parts of southern Ontario, Quebec and the Maritimes, people will be able to see the moon pass between the sun and the earth. And in those brief moments, day will turn to night. Niagara Falls, Ontario should be one of the best places to catch the celestial event. And preparations are in full swing for a surge of visitors. Tonight, Katie Nicholson gives us a glimpse into the region's readiness and a primer on staying safe. Here's a hint. These, they're in very short supply. What are you pairing with the eclipse? Gregory Virch's eclipse and Sips wine event sold out almost as soon as he floated the idea. In this vast tank Merlot ready to be served on the big day but he's worried his small winery will be overrun. So for us it's always an issue with say washroom capacities with parking spots and things like this so we're actually hiring or we're bringing and extra staff. So this is where we're going to try and set up for the eclipse. The Niagara region is in the path of totality. It'll have one of the best views of April 8th's solar eclipse. Everything is here that you could possibly need. Prompting skyrocketing demands for hotel rooms at Super Bowl prices. Some more than $1,000. Most of the folks that are here are from the United States, but we do have some from overseas. David Carroll oversees three hotels on the American side, three on the Canadian side, including this one, all pretty much sold out. I've been in the business a long time. I'm shocked the amount of people that are coming up to this part of the United States and Canada to see an eclipse for four minutes. I'm really surprised. But there is a calling for it, so people are excited. So I tested these during the day, and I couldn't see a thing. And perhaps no one is excited as the mayor of Niagara Falls. We're preparing for the worst, hoping for the best. We know there could end up being upwards of a million people in the city. The city has ordered tens of thousands of certified eclipse viewing glasses but still expects to run out. It's kind of like get a bigger boat in Jaws and that's kind of where we are. We're like get more glasses. Police have been coordinating on a safety plan anticipating hours of gridlock throughout the region. There is a mathematical number, so how many cars can be on the road in the Niagara region. If we overwhelm that, there's only so much we can do. They're asking people to plan ahead, make sure they have food, water and medication with them. Of course, if the weather is anything like this miserable March day, all that planning, the anticipation, the worry and those glasses for nothing.

If they did come back, they probably went full astern trying to stop the movement of the ship. And then they lost power again. Again, I think we must be ahead of the May Day in that they're still seeing traffic going across. And they did say they were able to stop traffic from crossing. What must have been happening on the bridge, seeing this looming? Yeah, very hectic on the bridge, obviously. Asking for the crew to run back, obviously, so that the crew doesn't get hurt. The crew that was looking after the anchors, after handling the anchors and the mooring lines after departure, and basically brace yourself, you know, because it's going to hit hard.
And you would know that, that it is coming at this time.
Yeah, there's not much they could have done at that time.
So, again, watching this, and we're just seconds away from that moment of impact,

and we have to be very sensitive because, of course, at this point, there are people who are alive on that bridge, and the impact is coming, and it is horrific. What would it have been like at the moment of impact on board? You know, it's on such a big ship, you know, the steel would have buckled, obviously, so it wouldn't be an impact like in a car crash or an airplane crash. It's kind of a soft stop for the crew of the vessel. It's just stunning. It was instant. The bridge came it was instant yeah the bridge came down yeah the pillar just gave up and that was it you know there was not much to do and now i mean just to stop to absorb that again no matter how many times we've seen that today it is unbelievable and now of course the questions and the investigation as you have, again, in real time with me, did you see anything that may suggest what they did wrong, or did they appear to have followed protocols from your expert eyes, but from afar, obviously? From what we know, I don't know much more than you do. From what we know, I think everything was done to try and minimize the impact, save lives, and I think the pilots, the captain, the crew did that, obviously. And the rescue workers, the police that stopped the traffic and helped stop the traffic. I personally don't think there's anything else that could have done to prevent that at that stage, obviously. Okay. Was the maintenance of the ship done properly and all that? That's just conjunctures. We don't know about that. But these are the questions for investigations which are already underway so as you said maintenance what are the kinds of things that they'll look like in terms of where the failures were well there's a vdr on board so there's a video data recorder like a black box on an airplane if you want so they will be listening to that to see what happened the chain of events so they'll see it and they'll hear the conversations they will yeah and is there also data being recorded as well? Just like in a plane crash. Exactly. So if there were things not going the way they should have been in the engine room, that the VDR might have recorded that. And then the TSB or the people doing the inquiry will have more details into what led to that blackout. And what about human contribution here or human error? Is that something they'll they'll look at they will definitely look into it I don't know if they'll find any but they'll definitely look into it for sure yeah what do you think might be the broader impact of this this is a rare event it's not an unprecedented event there have been dozens of these over the years but what impact could this have on other shipping companies and the you know the maritime industry? There will be an immediate local impact on Baltimore because it handles a lot of cargo. So, you know, at least I think there's about 40 ships stranded in the harbor right now that will not be able to get out of the harbor until they clear the pathway, the fairway. So there's going to be an economical impact for Baltimore for sure. And probably for the eastern seaboard, reshuffling all this cargo on the eastern seaboard between all the other ports, New York and all these ports. But will shipping companies, manufacturers, will they look to see if there's a problem that may affect other similar ships? Is that a question? We'll see what the TSB finds out. If there was a specific item that could be looked after on other ships, they will issue a warning and people will look into it. But I would be surprised. Blackouts do happen. It happened in an area, you know, the worst of timing, some sort of a perfect storm event. But, you know, if they find something that could be looked at on the other ships of the fleet, they will issue warnings that the ship owner can look into. But as you said, a perfect storm. And we have the catastrophic results. Thank you so much for sharing your expertise and really helping us understand everything better, Captain. You're welcome. Coming up, a Canadian journalist in Haiti on the worsening humanitarian conditions in a country under the control of violent gangs.
Nothing comes in and nothing comes out of Haiti right now.
Our conversation coming up next. a first-hand account of the growing crisis in Haiti.
Harold Isaac is a Canadian independent journalist who has reported from Haiti through nearly a decade of turmoil and change.
We've reached him in the capital, Port-au-Prince, for insight into the mounting misery.
Harold, welcome. Glad to meet you here tonight.
Thanks for having me, Heather.
I'm interested, first of all, in what you're seeing and reporting on to the ground right now.
How bad is the violence right now, Harold?
Well, the violence, at least the peak of violence we've had in the last month,

have kind of subsided. Now we're more in a phase of humanitarian catastrophe unfolding, especially with hunger that is picking up throughout town and folks trying to resupply as much as they can. Can we talk about that? Because we've been hearing that the criminal gangs who are controlling the capital are controlling the food supply.
How bad are the shortages for the Haitian people?
Well, to put it simply, really, nothing comes in and nothing comes out of Haiti right now.
And that's affecting both port facilities, the airport.
Port-au-Prince is, for the most part, cut off from the rest of the world.

So in terms of food actually getting to the people, mean are the shortages quite profound at this point well the way it's articulating right now you go to markets and supermarkets either you start getting uh producers that are rottening because people cannot afford them the prices are rising or you see empty shelves you know and uh and you know you don't see the products you would normally see because most of them are stuck in the port facilities and are not coming out because the gangs still control access to these facilities. Okay. And what about the airport? You mentioned that. What is the status of the airports now? Well, it's been over three weeks now since the airport has been shut down by gang violence and has yet to reopen. The only international airport functioning in Haiti is 250 kilometers up north in Cape Haitian, which is very hard to access for folks in Port-au-Prince. And that's hindering efforts to evacuate. Foreign nationals are stranded in Haiti, and it's also preventing humanitarian aid to come in into the country. That's part of the story we've been talking about here. We know there are about 3,000 Canadians who are registered in Haiti. Given what you're saying, how challenging is it going to be, Harold, to get them out? Well, for the most part, those who have been fortunate enough to be able to leave Haiti have done so through very hefty pricing helicopters. Choppers have taken folks out of the country, or they were provided by foreign governments such as the U.S. government, the French government, and now the Canadian government that have been supplying choppers to come and get nationals. But, you know, a chopper can only take so many people at once, and this is the only way out of Port-au-Prince as it stands, the only safe way out. The Canadians are just some of the thousands of people who must be trying to get out of Haiti right now. What is the impact, do you think, of that migration, broadly of that migration? Well, listen, Haiti has been in a very complicated situation for the past few years, mainly with both an institutional and a constitutional crisis that got aggravated with the assassination of President Jovenel Moise in July of 2021. Now, we are facing today, and at least for the last two weeks, a total void of governance in the country, which is making things complicated to address the whole situation, whether security, whether the humanitarian catastrophe unfolding. It's's a very very complicated situation uh and it's triggering you know migration movements as you just said um as you know people are losing hope and they're trying to find a way out trying to find a better future somewhere else so you mentioned the governance i mean there is a plan for a transitional council to replace the prime minister who resigned and to move Haiti toward elections in the future. But hearing just today that that seems to be, that whole plan seems to be in jeopardy. Again, what's the latest you understand of that, Harold? It is complicated. We were promised a government within 48 hours a few weeks ago, and it's been two weeks now. And there were supposed to be elections within the seven-member committee, presidential committee, presidential council yesterday, and that did not happen. And at least two of the stakeholders within that committee have asked for a delay of about 72 hours to try and reconciliate various contradictory positions within the committee.
So it's a very complicated process that's taking time,
and it's unclear whether or not it will be delivered in the coming week.
We'll have to definitely be following that closely.

I'm interested, finally, in your own situation. What are your own plans and what concerns do you have for your own personal safety, Harold? Well, I'm not just by myself. My family is here. My friends are here. Leaving is not yet an option for me, per se. Precisely, I think, I mean, besides the hefty cost of doing so and the uncertain future after that, but also because, you know, I have friends and family here. And leaving them stranded or in a sort of a predicament is something that would be unbearable to me personally, but I respect everybody's choices if they feel that they have to leave. Well, I wish you well as you stay put, and we'll be in touch hopefully in the future. Thank you very much, Harold, for that information and all of your insight tonight. Thanks for having me, Heather. And next, it's an Easter eggstravaganza. There's more eggs than people on the island. Meet the shop owner with a surplus of sweets. That's in our moment, next. well it's an early easter for a shop owner on the orkney island of sandy who went on a bit of a sweet shopping spree and now he has a problem he has more eggs to sell than people to buy them seems the yoke's on him and it's our moment i just felt totally embarrassed to be honest with you we run sinclair general stores we're located on a group of islands just north of scotland the whole island community is 500 people with kind of big holidays like christmas and easter you'll do like a pre-sale we thought we needed about 80 eggs and it didn't take long to realize that actually I'd ordered 80 cases as opposed to 80 individual eggs meaning we had 720 eggs there was more eggs than people on the island did you try and return them yeah absolutely got on the phone straight away can you take these back I've made a big mistake but they didn't want them back so here we are I'd be a very rich man if I had a pound for every person that came in and said, got any Easter eggs, mate?
